item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea and intention are nice. But it has some mild tone issues that do not sit right with me, so I'll downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-096, actually - "dangerously modest", removing eyes means you survive

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's nicely executed, but i'm really not a fan of Foundation-analogues, so no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Typically, if it's just a location being blackboxed, it's allowed to be in the Containment Procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Redaction in containment procedures. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely. A bit too modest, also removing eyes allows to survive the encounter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how it cross-references some SCPs, leaves room for imagination, and combines its old age with modern radios. An image that popped into my head was an ancient Foundation, using this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've specified in the containment procedures that it is contained in situ to prevent Event 1601-BR and to prevent further damage to the object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing something with this one. The log entries piqued my interest but ultimately couldn't hold it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, though only because it's still contained in situ for no real reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this could be really good with the right execution, but this isn't quite there yet. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've clarified the age restrictions and fixed the speech and frequency things. Thanks for your input. To answer your questions, it doesn't "know" which frequencies to use, it just happens to broadcast on some frequencies we currently use. No, there have been no writing systems like that, it's more of a security feature in it's original use to restrict who had access to the information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has indeed improved, though not quite there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At no time should any sentient SCP of indeterminate age be allowed within a 5 km radius of the object.

So if the age of the sentient SCP is known precisely then it's okay to get closer to the lantern? That doesn't make sense; I'd think that they just wouldn't let any sentient SCPs nearby.

After effort from Foundation linguists, the key to translation was discovered to be the depth and angle of the characters, which acted as both context and syntax clues.

Interesting. Have there ever been any actual writing systems like that, or is this just another way the SCP is strange?

If an entity that SCP-1601 has cataloged enters within 5 km of the lantern, SCP-1601 reacts by releasing a large-scale (307 km radius) radio burst on multiple bandwidths and frequencies,

1) "Frequencies" is redundant if "bandwidths" is used. Anyways, a technical document would probably just say "multiple frequencies".

2) Both AM and FM? And it knows which frequency ranges use which?

Although the text will likely make little sense to most listeners

"Although the speech will likely…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I read it as more of an ancient recording system for SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh this is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to think of some good excerpts instead of the description summaries.

Edit: OK, I added some excerpts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but there are two caveats. First, are those 'description summaries' the actual words, researchers' notes, or both? It's hard to tell. If it's the latter case, you may want to separate them out instead of using the parentheses, and if it's just researchers' notes (which an above comment seems to suggest), I think I'd rather see actual transcriptions, even if they're garbled.

Second, why the on-site containment? The article states that the base is non-active, so why not shear it off near the floor and transport it to a safe location with a Faraday cage?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. I mean, at least excerpts from the original descriptions. I mean, I was expecting those to be the hook of the article, and the summaries there fail to deliver.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object itself is interest, but after I peeled back the completely unnecessarily complicated description, I realized that all this is is an item that predicts or creates other SCPs. That is, unfortunately, extremely cliche and not very interesting at all, despite the decent execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What, you mean full log entries instead of summaries?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could be good if you had actually written authentic-sounding log entries from the lamp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or, are there other missing artifacts that could receive the broadcast?

To me, this seems like an Emergency Broadcast System for SCPs. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor quibble, would "proto-Japanese" date from an era before the introduction of the Chinese ideographs? If so, Japanese would likely not have a "A character translated as Location/Prison/Status," as it would still be syllable-based. (According to the almighty internets, the first known written form of Japanese came from the 7th century, around the time of the introduction of Chinese ideographs - but proto-Japanese dates from about 300 BC.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy the concept, but this current elaboration doesn't truly pique my interest (though it gets close). No vote yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many SCPs in one? That wins it for me. It has some interesting details, like being an old stone thing, yet emitting something that modern radios can use…

Only thing I don't like is the redaction in the title. If it's forbidden to know what catalog is it, then it could be just "The Catalog" or something… no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dangerously Modest Man of Heaven

SCP-343?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You might want to add the following:

Like all SCPs found in japan, it also has the power to transform into a giant robot in order to fight space monsters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I guess this could be another artifact from the same people, depending on how geographically spread they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me too much of SCP-557.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did. But I overlooked them because I read it over in the sandbox and I hadn't selected a number yet.

When I got it over, I neglected to do a search for them, although I have fixed that now. I'm sorry you don't like the tone and idea, though. What are the specifics, if you don't mind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know. Still should've been proofread. The sheer frequency of them shows that you did not give this a once over when posting to the site.

Despite that, there are also a few glaring tone issues, and the idea is meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The XXXXs are gone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for SCP-XXXXs (should've been proofread), and poor tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dangerously Modest Man of Heaven

…why do I have the sneaking suspicion that this is SCP-096?

This is a cute little concept. I'll give it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another "thing that random shit comes out of". Yawn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok. I think, with tprince, I got all of the XXXXs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always remember when you post an SCP to edit the XXXX to a number. Otherwise, good job.
